title: Jupiter and Semele
description: Moreau's monumental vision of the myth in which the mortal Semele is consumed by the unveiled radiance of Jupiter is an overwhelming accumulation of jewel-like detail. The god sits enthroned amid a phantasmagoric architecture of encrusted ornament, while Semele swoons at the moment of her transfiguration. The painting is a cathedral of color — deep blues, burnished golds, and flickering reds layered into an almost hallucinatory density.

title: The Apparition
description: In this haunting watercolor, the severed head of John the Baptist appears floating in a halo of light before a dancing Salome, who points at it in fascinated horror. The architecture of Herod's palace dissolves into an ornate fantasy of Moorish arches and incense-laden atmosphere. Moreau creates a dreamworld where the sacred and the sensual merge in shimmering, translucent washes of color.

title: The Cyclops
medium: Oil on cardboard mounted on panel
description: Redon transforms the myth of Polyphemus and Galatea into a tender, dreamlike vision. The enormous one-eyed giant peers over a ridge of wildflowers at the sleeping nymph below, his expression more wistful than threatening. The landscape blooms with iridescent color — violet rocks, emerald slopes, and flowers in impossible hues — creating a world governed entirely by the logic of dreams.

title: Ophelia Among the Flowers
description: Redon's Ophelia is not Shakespeare's drowned maiden but a luminous presence dissolved into a field of radiant wildflowers. Her profile emerges from a cascade of blossoms rendered in Redon's extraordinary pastel technique — soft, glowing colors that seem lit from within. The boundary between figure and garden dissolves, suggesting the continuity between consciousness and nature that fascinated the Symbolists.

title: Isle of the Dead
description: The most reproduced painting of the late nineteenth century, Böcklin's vision of a rocky island tomb rising from dark, still waters struck a universal chord of melancholy. A white-shrouded figure stands in a small boat being rowed toward the island's cypress-guarded entrance. The oppressive silence of the scene is conveyed through the painting's muted, funereal palette and the absolute stillness of the glassy water.

title: War
description: Four apocalyptic horsemen thunder through a burning landscape on monstrous steeds, trampling the dead beneath them. Böcklin's nightmarish allegory of war anticipates the catastrophes of the twentieth century with terrifying prescience. The palette is dominated by sulphurous yellows, blood reds, and the black smoke of devastation, creating an image of almost medieval ferocity.

title: The Caress
location: Royal Museums of Fine Arts of Belgium, Brussels
description: A leopard-bodied sphinx with the head of a beautiful woman presses her cheek against the face of a young androgynous figure in this enigmatic allegory of desire and danger. Khnopff's hyper-refined technique renders every detail with photographic precision yet the effect is profoundly dreamlike. The cool, muted palette of sand, blue, and spotted fur creates an atmosphere of frozen, dangerous intimacy.
